rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 20, 2014, Line 1, PLAINTIFF JESSICA JIMENEZ (Amended) Motion To Compel Defendnat's To Provide Further Responses To Plaintiff's Request For Production Of Documents, Set One; To Compel Defendant's To Produce Responsive Documents, And Request For Monetary Sanctions Of At Least $3680.00 Against Defendant's. Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Moving party has not replied to defendant's statement that it will be producing the documents at issue. Parties to meet and confer and then appear to discuss when such documents will be produced and if sanction are appropriate. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THERESA LYNNE MURPHY VS. SAN FRANCISCO COUNTY ASSESOR-RECORDER, PHIL TING et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Clerical Correction Of Judgment Filed In Dept 302 On 1/27/12
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 20, 2014, Line 3, PLAINTIFF THERESA MURPHY'S Motion For Clerical Correction Of Judgment Filed In Dept 302 On 1/27/12. Denied. A final judgment may only be altered where the Court has made a "clerical mistake." (CCP sec. 473(d).) "The test which distinguishes clerical error from possible judicial error is simply whether the challenged portion of the judgment was entered inadvertently (which is clerical error) versus advertently (which might be judicial error, but is not clerical error). Unless the challenged portion of the judgment was entered inadvertently, it cannot be changed post judgment under the guise of correction of clerical error." (Bell v. Farmers Ins. Exch. (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1138, 1144.) Plaintiff has not established that the Court inadvertently failed to include the language she seeks to add in the amended judgment. The only cause of action at issue in the motion for summary judgment was to quiet title. The motion for summary judgment did not address the two deeds of trust at issue. (See Defendant JP Morgan Chase Bank, N.A.'s Request for Judicial Notice, Ex. 13.) A demurrer had previously been sustained without leave to amend to the cancellation of written instrument cause of action. As such, the Court can infer that the final judgment entered in this case purposefully did not address the deeds of trust and that no clerical error was committed. All requests for judicial notice are granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: IDA CHRISTINA FUA CRUZ VS. JOEL ENRIQUE ANDINO SANCHEZ et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Responses To Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 20, 2014, Line 2, Motion To Compel Responses To Discovery. (Part 2 of 3) (4) special interrogatories to defendant Taxi Property and Wolley: when will supplemental responses be provided to interrogatories #6-8, what is relevance of interrogatory #16; interrogatories #17- 22 may be relevant, however, which corporation is reference being made to?; with respect to interrogatories #23-26, when will supplemental responses be made; with respect to interrogatories #27-45, have the applicable articles, bylaws and/or operating agreements been produced?; with respect to interrogatories #46-47, when will supplemental responses to be provided and why the limitation from 2006-2007?; with respect to interrogatories #48-57 need to discuss relationship to alter ego in light of Yellow Cab's audited financial statements; interrogatories #58-62, when will supplemental response be provided; with respect to interrogatories #63-73 need to discuss relationship to alter ego in light of audited financial statements; interrogatories #74-85, why is the existence of such a permit not relevant to the subject matter of alter ego?; interrogatories #86-106 not clear who you or who shares of stock are being referred to and have the minute books, share registry and other documents been produced?; with respect to interrogatories #107-112, when will supplemental responses to be provided and why the limitation from 2006-2007?; interrogatory #113-123, these may be relevant but overbroad and need redaction; with respect to interrogatories #124-151, need to discuss relationship to alter ego in light of Yellow Cab's audited financial statements; interrogatories #151-182, appear to be discoverable, but may be overbroad and unnecessary in light of Yellow Cab's audited financial statement; interrogatory #183 may be relevant to the subject matter but interrogatories #184-186 need not be answered due to tax priviledge; interrogatory 187 may be discoverable, but interrogatories #188-190 need not be answered due to tax priviledge; interrogatories #191-220, appear to be relevant to the subject matter but may be overbroad and unnecessary in light of Yellow Cab's audited financial statement; interrogatories #221-224 are not discoverable; interrogatories #225-280, appear to be relevant to the subject matter, but may be overbroad and unnecessary in light of Yellow Cab's audited financial statement; interrogatory 281 was answered and interrogatories 282-283 are also answered. (5) request for production to individual defendants: #1 - when will policies be produced?; #6-15 are privileged tax records; #16 - why isn't this discoverable; #17-20 are satisfactory as no documents apparently exist; #21-22 are privileged tax records; #23-24 these records appear violate the right of privacy and more than relevance is required. (Britt v. Superior Ct. (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 859); #25-28 are satisfactory as no documents apparently exist; #29-32 while some documents may be relevant to the subject matter as framed it is overbroad and burdensome; #33 when will supplemental response be provided; #34-36, technically there is no stock, however, to avoid further discovery perhaps defendants would agree to produce documents relating ownership interest; #37-56 are satisfactory as no documents apparently exist; #57-58 - all nonprivilged documents to be produced. (6) request for production to Wolley, Taxi Property and Yellow Cab: court cannot determine what specifically is at issue (Continued to 3 of 3).

(Britt v. Superior Ct. (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 859); when will supplemental responses to interrogatories 71-90 be provided? (Continued to 2 of 3).

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTOPHER CALVI VS. VITO J. LBERATI et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Attendance Of Deft Vito J Liberati At Deposition To Compel The Production Of Documents And Things And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 20, 2014, Line 3, DEFENDANT SILVER BAY SEAFOODS LLC Motion To Compel Attendance Of Defendant Vito J Liberati At Deposition To Compel The Production Of Documents And Things And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: One has the right to obtain discovery from a debtor like any third party. (See In re Hillsborough Holdings Corp. (MD Fla. 1991) 130 B.R. 603, 605; In re Beeney (9th Cir. BAP 1992) 142 B.R. 360, 363.) The court does not believe it has jurisdiction to compel a defendant who is in bankruptcy to submit to a deposition pursuant to a notice of deposition rather than a subpoena. In re Miller (9th Cir. BAP 2001) 262 B.R. 499 involved a subpoena which the state court failed to enforce. (Id. at 501-502.) A notice of deposition is based on the debtor deponent being a party to the action and appears to be a form of process against the debtor. (Id. at 505; compare CCP §2025.280 with §2020.010.) The automatic stay prevents continuation of actions against the debtor until there is relief from stay. (11 USC §362.) The filing of the bankruptcy divests state courts of jurisdiction. (Issacs v. Hobb Tie & Timber Co. (1931) 282 U.S. 734, 739.) While it may seem like a technicality, it appears that once a defendant debtor is protected by the automatic stay, the proper procedure is to either get relief from the stay in the bankruptcy or subpoena the witness for deposition rather than obtain the advantage of using procedures that are designed solely for parties to the action. This will also avoid further proceedings in either this court or the bankruptcy court to address this issue by way of contempt or violation of the automatic stay. Moving party is encouraged to appear to address these issues, but if no appearance is made, motion will be denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK L PERLMUTTER VS. EDUARDO ALARCON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: First Amended Mtn To Dismiss
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 20, 2014, Line 9, DEFENDANT EDUARDO ALARCON'S First Amended Motion To Dismiss. Denied. Defendant's motion sets forth no legal basis for dismissing the case. Defendant's motion does not conform to the requirements for a motion for summary judgment set forth in CCP sec. 437c. Defendant sets forth no admissible evidence in support of his contention that California lacks personal jurisdiction over him. rulings.Case Title: TIARA BILLUPS LARKIN, AN INDIVIDUAL VS. AYLSSA GUTNER-DAVIS, AN INDIVIDUAL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compel Pltf'S Attendance And Testimony At Depo
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 20, 2014, Line 5, DEFENDANT AYLSSA GUTNER-DAVIS, Motion To Compel Plaintiff's Attendance And Testimony At Deposition. Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted no opposition. Plaintiff to appear at a deposition at a date and time to be agreed to by the parties within 15 days of service of this order otherwise it will be set by defense counsel. Sanctions of $580 to be paid by December 22, 2014. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rkane1089@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HVCH LP, VS. PACIFIC INSTITUTE, A CALIFORNIA NON-PROFIT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 20, 2014, Line 16, DEFENDANTS NADER SHABAHANGI, PACIFIC INSTITUTE, AGESONG, INC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2) The Special Demurrer is overruled. The parties in this action are not the same parties as those in Shabahangi v. Shabahangi (SF Sup. Ct. Case NO. CGC-11-508919), which is fatal to Defendants' request under CCP § 430.10(c), and Defendants' apparent attempt in reply to abandon the request pursuant to CCP § 430.10(c) and instead seek a stay under CRC 3.515 is also denied. No party has petitioned for coordination of this or the earlier matter. Next, none of the causes of action are barred by the statute of limitations because the Complaint sufficiently alleges that Plaintiff did not have a reason to discover the breach until Plaintiff received financial documents in connection with another lawsuit. The Demurrer is sustained with 10 days leave to amend as to the breach of contract claim because the Complaint fails to allege that all required conditions were satisfied. Plaintiff's allegation that "all conditions precedent to the payments . . . had been met" is insufficient due to the qualifying language. Plaintiff also has leave to amend in order to include the concededly missing pages of the agreement. The Demurrer is sustained with 10 days leave to amend as to the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action. First, the Complaint sufficiently alleges the existence of a fiduciary relationship. Second, the Complaint sufficiently alleges conduct that, if true, amounts to a breach of fiduciary duty. However, the inconsistent allegations as to Defendant Shabahangi's status as a board member and/or his authority to control Pacific Institute renders the Complaint uncertain and confusing, and does not sufficiently alert the Defendants as to the charges asserted against them. Next, the Demurrer is overruled as to the causes of action for fraud and misrepresentation as against Defendant Shabahangi. However, the Demurrer is sustained with leave to amend as to these causes of action against Defendant Pacific Institute as the Complaint is unclear as to whether anyone with authority to act for Pacific Institute made misrepresentations. The Demurrer is overruled as to the cause of action for conspiracy because there is a viable claim for fraud as the Complaint sufficiently the required elements. The Demurrer is overruled as to the claims for constructive and resulting trusts. While constructive and resulting trusts are in fact remedies, they may be pled in the Complaint to allege a right to seek such remedies. Finally, the Demurrer is overruled as to the causes of action for interference with prospective economic advantage as the Complaint meets the pleading requirements for these claims. (See Korea Supply Co. v. Lockheed Martin Corp. (2003) 29 Cal.4th 1134, 1153 [plaintiff is not required to plead that defendant acted with the specific intent, or purpose, of disrupting plaintiff's prospective economic advantage; instead, it is sufficient to plead that defendant knew that the interference was certain or substantially certain to occur.].) (see Part 2) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: HVCH LP, VS. PACIFIC INSTITUTE, A CALIFORNIA NON-PROFIT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 20, 2014, Line 16, DEFENDANTS NADER SHABAHANGI, PACIFIC INSTITUTE, AGESONG, INC.'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. The Motion to Strike is granted in part and denied in part. The Motion to Strike the request for a permanent injunction is granted with 10 days leave to amend as it fails to demonstrate why there is an inadequate remedy at law. The Motion to Strike the request for punitive damages is denied as moot as to the cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty and as to the cause of action for fraud against Defendant Pacific Institute in light of the ruling on Defendants' Demurrer. The Motion is denied as to the cause of action for fraud as against Defendant Shabahangi as there is a viable claim for fraud that supports a prayer for punitive damages. The Motion is denied as to the cause of action for conspiracy against both Defendants. The Memorandum of Points and Authorities moves only on the grounds that there is no evidence to support this relief. However, evidence is not required at the pleading stage. The Motion is granted as to the causes of action for resulting and constructive trusts. A constructive trust is available where the specific res or funds can be identified and attached, "but not where the plaintiff seeks to impose general personal liability as a remedy for the defendant's monetary obligations." (Honolulu Joint Apprenticeship and Training Committee of United Ass'n Local Union No. 675 v. Foster (9th Cir. 2003) 332 F.3d 1234, 1238.) The Complaint here alleges that Defendant Shabahangi diverted Pacific Institute funds and used them to pay for personal expenses. (Compl. Paragraph 14.) Thus, the Complaint's clear allegations are that at least some of the money at issue is no longer in Defendants' possession, and therefore the Complaint fails to allege that particular funds in Defendants' current possession can be traced to money belonging to Plaintiff.
